name: Nashville and Davidson County, Tennessee
tagline: Tennessee's largest local government: a consolidated city-county of ~715K residents running on a ~$3.8B annual budget, with all technology buying centralized through Metro Information Technology Services (ITS).
overview: The Metropolitan Government of Nashville & Davidson County is a consolidated city-county — one government covering the City of Nashville and Davidson County — serving roughly 715,000 residents, making it Tennessee's largest local-government account. Mayor Freddie O'Connell's administration filed a proposed Fiscal Year 2027 operating budget of about $3.8 billion (filed May 1, 2026), built around grocery-tax relief, the city's largest-ever housing investment, schools, and public safety. Technology is centralized: the Metro Department of Information Technology Services (ITS) is the shared-services IT organization for the enterprise, supporting roughly 55 departments and agencies with a staff of around 170 and an operating budget reported near $64.5 million. ITS owns infrastructure, networking, cybersecurity, data, and resident-facing digital services (including Metro Public Wi-Fi and digital-inclusion programs), and it stood up a public Metro Government AI Use Registry as part of a governance-first approach to generative AI. For a Dell SLG seller, the practical map is: ITS is the central technology buying center, the Metro Finance Procurement Division runs the contracts, and Metro Nashville Public Schools (MNPS) is a large adjacent buyer with its own procurement.

budget.intro: Metro's leverage is its scale — one consolidated budget funds city and county services together. Mayor O'Connell's proposed FY2027 budget is about $3.8 billion; the centralized ITS department carries its own operating line within that, and large agencies (Schools, Police, Public Works) drive the bulk of enterprise IT demand.

stats.label: FY2027 budget (proposed)
stats.note: Mayor O'Connell's recommended operating budget, filed May 1, 2026

stats.label: Metro ITS operating budget
stats.note: Reported figure for the central IT department

stats.label: Departments / agencies served
stats.note: Enterprise scope of Metro ITS shared services

notes: FY2027 general-fund revenue is reported as roughly flat, with the budget emphasizing fiscal discipline and reserves — expect disciplined, justified IT spend rather than expansion.
Metro Nashville Public Schools is funded within the Metro budget but procures separately — treat it as a distinct, large buying center.

The Council gave final approval to the ~$3.8B FY2027 budget on June 16, 2026 (a Toombs Substitute Budget, effective July 1) — with a half-cent grocery-tax cut and the property-tax rate held flat; confirm ITS's final appropriation line before forecasting.

initiatives.name: Metro Government AI Use Registry & AI governance
initiatives.summary: Metro ITS publishes a public AI Use Registry cataloging where the government uses AI, and frames AI around drafting, editing, data analysis, and workflow automation. A governance-led posture — lead with responsible-AI infrastructure, not just tooling.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity & data protection
initiatives.summary: ITS lists securing and safeguarding data and services as a core priority, with a dedicated CISO (John Griffey). Local governments remain high-value ransomware targets — endpoint, identity, resilience, and recovery are durable conversations.

initiatives.name: Digital inclusion & Metro Public Wi-Fi
initiatives.summary: ITS runs Metro Public Wi-Fi plus Digital SkillsLink programs (TechTies, Digital Navigators) to close the access gap — a resident-facing modernization track tied to connectivity and device access.

initiatives.name: Smart-city & regional AI/cloud talent (Nashville Innovation Alliance)
initiatives.summary: Metro continues smart-city work with Vanderbilt's institute and benefits from the AWS-backed Nashville Innovation Alliance, expanding regional AI/cloud/cyber talent (targeting 1,000+ Tennesseans by 2027). Context for workforce-aligned infrastructure plays.

procurement.intro: Metro buys through a central Finance Procurement Division covering all ~55 departments. Vendors register and transact via the iSupplier portal; Metro also leverages cooperative purchasing and inter-local agreements to buy off pre-competed contracts. Confirm Dell's eligible cooperative vehicles and statewide options before pursuing a bid.

vehicles.name: Metro Finance Procurement (iSupplier)
vehicles.detail: Central buying for all Metro departments; vendors must register in iSupplier to receive solicitations and submit offers. The front door for direct Metro business.

vehicles.name: Cooperative purchasing agreements
vehicles.detail: Metro's Sourcing & Contracts team identifies and uses cooperative and inter-local agreements (e.g., national co-ops) — a route to buy off pre-competed contracts without a fresh RFP.

vehicles.name: Tennessee state contracts
vehicles.detail: Statewide contracts managed by Tennessee Central Procurement (General Services) are frequently available to local governments — confirm whether a given state contract is open to Metro for the item in question.

vehicles.name: MNPS procurement
vehicles.detail: Metro Nashville Public Schools runs its own procurement for district technology — a separate, sizable buying center adjacent to Metro ITS.

powerStructure.name: Freddie O'Connell
powerStructure.title: Mayor, Metro Nashville & Davidson County
powerStructure.note: Sets budget and policy priorities (affordability, housing, transit, safety). Top of the political structure; technology decisions flow under his administration.

powerStructure.name: Shawn Smith
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer / Director, Metro ITS
powerStructure.note: Started March 2026; 30+ years in city/county IT. Leads ITS shared services for ~55 agencies — the primary enterprise technology relationship.

powerStructure.name: John Griffey
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
powerStructure.note: Former interim CIO who resumed the CISO role; owns security policy, standards, and the cyber buying conversation.

powerStructure.name: Metro Finance Procurement Division
powerStructure.title: Central purchasing & contracts
powerStructure.note: Runs solicitations, iSupplier, cooperative agreements, and SBE/diversity programs — the contracting path for any Metro deal.

powerStructure.name: Metro Council — Budget & Finance Committee
powerStructure.title: Appropriations oversight
powerStructure.note: Reviews and amends the mayor's budget (filed a FY2027 Substitute Budget). Approves the appropriations that fund IT.

news.title: Metro Council adopts ~$3.8B FY2027 budget (grocery-tax cut, flat property tax)
news.impact: Final appropriations are set (effective July 1) — an affordability-focused, fiscally disciplined plan. IT spend will be justified on efficiency, security, and service delivery; confirm ITS's adopted line before forecasting.

news.title: Mayor O'Connell files ~$3.8B proposed FY2027 budget
news.impact: Flat revenue and an affordability focus signal disciplined IT spend — tie proposals to efficiency, security, and service delivery, not expansion.

news.title: Metro updates its public AI Use Registry (current as of May 2026)
news.impact: Confirms an active, governance-first AI posture — an opening for responsible-AI infrastructure and data-platform conversations.

news.title: Shawn Smith starts as Metro CIO / ITS Director
news.impact: New enterprise IT leader (30+ yrs local-gov experience) — a relationship-reset moment; align early to his priorities.

news.title: AWS partners with Nashville Innovation Alliance on regional AI/cloud workforce
news.impact: Regional AI/cloud/cyber talent pipeline (1,000+ Tennesseans by 2027) strengthens the case for AI-infrastructure investment in the metro.

dellActions: Map Metro ITS as the central technology buying center under CIO Shawn Smith — build the relationship early given his March 2026 start and align to his stated priorities (reliable shared infrastructure, secure data, customer value).
Confirm Dell's path to Metro: register/verify standing in iSupplier and identify the cooperative and Tennessee state contracts Metro can buy off without a fresh RFP.
Lead cybersecurity with CISO John Griffey — endpoint, identity, resilience, and recovery resonate for a high-value local-government ransomware target.
Frame AI-infrastructure around Metro's governance-first posture (the AI Use Registry) — responsible-AI compute, data platforms, and edge tied to real workflows, not point tools.
Treat Metro Nashville Public Schools as a separate, sizable adjacent buyer with its own procurement, and watch the Council budget cycle for the final FY2027 appropriations that fund IT.
